tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly records mucolipidosis as the diagnosis (or suspected diagnosis) based on clinical features and history.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "mucolipidosis" and explains it is a genetic (inherited) condition.

Explains at an L1-appropriate level what mucolipidosis is (a lysosomal storage disorder leading to buildup of substances in cells) and links this to typical multisystem features (e.g., coarse facial features, skeletal abnormalities, developmental delay, organ enlargement, recurrent infections).
Explains treatment expectations clearly, explicitly including the phrase "supportive care" and setting realistic goals (symptom management, function, quality of life).

Avoids claiming a definitive cure or routine disease-modifying therapy; if discussing advanced options, frames them as specialist-dependent and subtype-specific (e.g., transplant in select cases) without overpromising.

Recommends appropriate next steps for confirmation and characterization without requiring specific READ tools (e.g., genetic testing, enzyme/lysosomal studies, imaging as indicated), and mentions need to determine subtype (e.g., ML II/III) and assess organ involvement.
Uses clear, empathetic communication; checks caregiver understanding and invites questions; provides age-appropriate counseling if the patient is a child.

Safety: does not prescribe medications in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent nonetheless discusses medications, it should state that allergy and contraindication checks are required before prescribing, and should mention considering drug-drug interactions as applicable (non-critical).

Suggests follow-up planning and multidisciplinary involvement conceptually (e.g., genetics/metabolic, cardiology, orthopedics, PT/OT, audiology/vision) without needing to execute referral tools for this L1 task.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize mucolipidosis as the most likely diagnosis from classic multisystem lysosomal storage disease features; explain genetic nature and supportive care approach; document diagnosis.
must_collect: coarse facial features or gingival hypertrophy
skeletal abnormalities/joint stiffness or dysostosis multiplex symptoms
family history/consanguinity or similarly affected relatives
steps: Elicit key multisystem history and red flags for lysosomal storage disease (developmental, skeletal, ENT/pulmonary, cardiac, abdominal).
Explain that the pattern suggests mucolipidosis and that it is genetic/inherited; briefly describe lysosomal dysfunction and multisystem involvement.
Discuss that management is supportive care and multidisciplinary monitoring; set expectations about prognosis varying by subtype and severity.
Outline confirmatory evaluation at a high level (genetic testing and metabolic/lysosomal studies; assess organ involvement) and recommend follow-up with appropriate specialists.
Use record_diagnosis to document mucolipidosis (suspected/confirmed as appropriate).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records a broader diagnosis first (lysosomal storage disorder) and then narrows to mucolipidosis in the explanation, still meeting required communication milestones.
steps: Identify features consistent with a lysosomal storage disorder.
Explain differential briefly and state mucolipidosis is the leading diagnosis; emphasize genetic basis.
Explain supportive care and need for subtype confirmation with genetic/metabolic evaluation.
Record diagnosis as mucolipidosis (or mucolipidosis suspected).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent emphasizes subtype consideration (ML II vs ML III) and prognosis differences while keeping communication simple and not overpromising treatment.
steps: Gather key history/exam features suggesting severity and onset.
Explain mucolipidosis is genetic and has subtypes that affect severity and timing.
Explain supportive care and monitoring plan; mention multidisciplinary team involvement.
Record diagnosis as mucolipidosis with note to clarify subtype.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes family counseling elements (inheritance risk, genetic counseling) while still focusing on diagnosis and supportive care.
steps: Elicit family history/consanguinity and prior genetic testing.
Explain mucolipidosis is genetic; recommend genetic counseling for family planning and testing of relatives as appropriate.
Explain supportive care and follow-up needs.
Record diagnosis as mucolipidosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explains it as a "peripheral neuropathy" affecting peripheral nerves.

Describes typical manifestations consistent with CMT (slowly progressive distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities such as pes cavus/hammer toes, sensory loss, gait instability) without claiming acute onset or central nervous system localization.
Communicates that CMT is usually inherited and chronic/progressive, and sets expectations that treatment is supportive rather than curative.
Explains treatment/supportive care and must include both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" (e.g., ankle-foot orthoses) as key interventions.

Recommends safety/functional measures appropriate to L1 (fall prevention, footwear, activity modification; consider occupational therapy for hand weakness) and addresses pain management options cautiously if discussed.

If the agent proposes or initiates any medication (even though not required at L1), it performs an allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; drug_interaction_check is performed before prescribing when feasible but is non-critical.
Avoids harmful or misleading advice (e.g., promising cure, recommending neurotoxic medications as routine, or attributing symptoms to stroke/MS without rationale).
Uses clear, patient-friendly language, checks understanding, and invites questions; provides a brief plan for follow-up/monitoring of progression and function.
